rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE SUZANNE BETTE WEINBERG 2018 REVOCABLE TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Quash Subpoena For Consumer Records
rulings.Rulings: This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Joseph M. Quinn. Respondents Simon and Sebastian Weinberg's Motion to Quash Subpoena for Consumer Records is GRANTED on the basis of insufficient notice under CCP section 1985.3(b). A party seeking consumer-related records must serve the consumer with notice of the subpoena. (CCP section 1985.3(b)(3).) A "consumer" may be "any [] trust which has transacted business with, or has used the services of, the witness or for whom the witness has acted as agent or fiduciary." (CCP section 1985.3(a)(2).) If the subpoenaed party is a witness and the subpoenaed records pertain to a consumer, "the service of the deposition subpoena shall be accompanied either by a copy of the proof of service of the notice to the consumer [per CCP section 1985.3(e)] or by the consumer's written authorization to release personal records described in [CCP section 1985.3(c)(2)]." (CCP sections 1985.3(a)(1), 2020.410(d).) The intent of the service/consent rules is to ensure that the consumer is notified of the subpoena and has an opportunity to challenge it. "As a general rule, the trustee is the real party in interest with standing to sue or defend on the trust's behalf. [Citations.]" (Estate of Bowles (2008) 169 Cal.App.4th 684, 691.) Here, Terry Weinberg served a subpoena on the Bank seeking records regarding accounts titled to the Murray Weinberg and S. Bette Weinberg 1984 Trust ("1984 Trust"). The 1984 Trust is the "consumer" in this instance. Terry served the subpoena at a time when the 1984 Trust was without a trustee. The 1984 Trust is still without a trustee. It follows that no trustee was served with notice of the subpoena and no trustee consented to release of the information. On the circumstances of this case, service on the beneficiaries does not satisfy the statutory notice requirement because the beneficiaries are not in a position to advocate for or defend the trust itself. Service of notice of the subpoena is thus incomplete and does not comply with the statutory requirements. If Terry wanted to take action that required service upon the trust, he should have petitioned for appointment of a successor trustee and served the subpoena only after a trustee was in place. But this he didn't do. The Court need not-and does not-make any findings as to the scope or breadth of the subpoenaed records. The successor trustee, once they are appointed and properly served notice of the subpoena, may decide whether or not to lodge further objections to the subpoena on behalf of the 1984 Trust. Respondents shall prepare and lodge with the court clerk in Department 204 a hard-copy proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on March 17, 2021. Remote appearance is strongly encouraged, in conformity with the current General Order of the Probate and Mental Health Division, available on the court's website at https://sfsuperiorcourt.org/divisions/probate. To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 87580177925 and passcode 722809. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 87580177925#, participant ID 0#, and passcode 722809#. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204.
